top_comments.text:
Some day I will write this up for real, but without going into detail, here's a summary.

The camera in Crash was on a rail. It could rotate left, right, up, and down (in Crash 2 and beyond, at least), but could not translate except by moving forward/backward on the rail. This motivates a key insight: if you're only rotating the camera, the sort order of the polygons in the scene cannot change.

This allowed us to sample points on the rail and render the frame at each sample poi

There's a lot of cheap crap on the market but there's also some great designs out there.

For example, I've got this light control system installed in my home:

http://houm.io/

Wireless, battery-free buttons that you can put anywhere you like. I keep one of them on my night stand. Rest of the switches look and work just like regular light switches.

Except the switches are connected to the system, so I can activate p

contrarian_analysis.bull_case_flaw: Pre Skene's core value proposition—plug-and-play growth automation via AI prompts in your codebase—assumes developers will trust and adopt yet another layer in their stack, but offers little evidence of deep, sticky integration or clear differentiation from existing analytics and PLG tools.
contrarian_analysis.elephant_in_room: How is Skene meaningfully better than simply wiring together Segment, Mixpanel, and a modern LLM agent, and why should devs trust a black-box AI to instrument critical growth logic directly in production code?

timing_assessment.reasoning: PLG automation and AI-powered dev tools are hot, but the market is crowded and developer trust in AI-driven code manipulation is still nascent; Skene risks being too early for mass adoption and too late to outmaneuver entrenched analytics/automation platforms.

incumbent_threat.most_dangerous_competitor: OpenAI (via GitHub Copilot/Actions) or Microsoft (VS Code ecosystem)
incumbent_threat.killer_feature: If Copilot or Google launches 'Growth Patterns'—AI-generated, code-aware onboarding/retention/freemium recipes natively in the IDE or cloud console, deeply integrated with analytics and billing APIs—Skene's standalone prompts become obsolete overnight.

hidden_assumptions: Developers will trust and adopt AI-generated growth automation in their codebase.
AI prompts can reliably handle the nuance and edge cases of real-world PLG workflows.
Teams want to replace, not just augment, their existing analytics and growth stacks.
The cost and friction of onboarding Skene is lower than stitching together best-of-breed tools.

moat_reality.claimed_moat: AI-powered, code-contextual growth automation with a large, ready-to-use prompt library for 381+ tools.
moat_reality.actual_moat: Prompt libraries and code-gen workflows are easy to replicate; nothing prevents incumbents or open source from copying the approach, and Skene lacks proprietary data or platform lock-in.

contrarian_analysis.honest_take: Skene is a clever bundling of PLG playbooks and AI code-gen, but its defensibility is paper-thin and the trust leap required from developers is non-trivial. Unless they show real traction or unique integrations, they're a feature, not a company.

primary_trigger.trigger: Recent advances in AI-powered workflow automation and LLM integration enable seamless onboarding and retention automation with minimal developer input.
primary_trigger.evidence: The rise of specialized LLM APIs and AI workflow platforms in 2023-2024, with open-source and commercial tools making integration easier and more reliable.

secondary_triggers.trigger: SaaS companies are under pressure to reduce churn and accelerate user activation as funding tightens and growth slows.
secondary_triggers.relevance: Automated onboarding and retention tools are now mission-critical for customer success and revenue growth.

secondary_triggers.trigger: Competitors like Pendo and Appcues are still heavily reliant on manual setup and limited AI capabilities.
secondary_triggers.relevance: Skene can differentiate by offering deeper automation and less developer overhead, capturing market share from legacy tools.

secondary_triggers.trigger: Product teams are shifting to leaner structures, demanding tools that automate repetitive work so developers can focus on core features.
secondary_triggers.relevance: Skene directly addresses the pain of limited engineering resources by automating growth and retention tasks.

why_now.why_not_earlier: Two years ago, AI workflow automation was unreliable and required heavy customization—recent LLM improvements and API standardization now make plug-and-play automation feasible for onboarding and retention.
why_now.why_not_later: Delaying would allow incumbents to catch up with AI features and entrench their customer bases, making it harder for Skene to differentiate and capture early adopters.
why_now.newsletter_hook: AI-powered automation just made onboarding and retention effortless—Skene is seizing the moment before legacy tools catch up.

takeaways.title: Embed Growth Logic as Code, Not UI
takeaways.insight: Treat growth infrastructure (activation, retention, gating) as versioned code within your repo, not as external scripts or UI overlays. This ensures transparency, developer ownership, and easier auditing.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Design growth flows as code modules or agents that interact with your application's logic. Integrate these directly into your repo and CI/CD pipeline, allowing updates via code pushes.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If your product is non-technical or cannot expose internal code for compliance reasons, this approach may not be feasible.
takeaways.example:
growth_agent.py

class GrowthAgent:
    def activate_user(self, user):
        # Activation logic here
        pass

# Hook into onboarding flow
on_user_signup.connect(GrowthAgent().activate_user)

takeaways.title: Prompt-Driven RAG for Targeted LLM Usage
takeaways.insight: Use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with highly specific prompts to minimize unnecessary LLM calls and reduce token usage. Tailor prompts to the exact context, leveraging a curated knowledge base.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Segment your knowledge base by tech stack and use context-aware retrieval to feed only relevant docs to the LLM. Cache prompt outputs for common flows.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If your use case requires fully generative, open-ended responses (e.g., creative writing), RAG may limit output diversity.
takeaways.example:
query = 'How to add freemium gating in Django + Stripe?'
docs = vector_db.search(query)
response = llm.generate(prompt=build_prompt(docs, query))

takeaways.title: Agentic Architectures for Parallel Growth Tasks
takeaways.insight: Implement agent-based systems to run multiple growth interventions (e.g., onboarding, retention nudges) in parallel, reducing latency and improving throughput for codebase analysis and updates.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Design agents as independent processes or threads, each responsible for a specific growth task. Use event-driven triggers to coordinate actions.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If your growth logic is tightly coupled or sequence-dependent, parallelization may introduce race conditions.
takeaways.example:
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

def run_growth_tasks(tasks):
    with ThreadPoolExecutor() as executor:
        executor.map(lambda t: t.run(), tasks)

takeaways.title: IDE-Integrated Growth Agents
takeaways.insight: Integrate growth automation directly into developer IDEs (e.g., Cursor, Windsurf) so interventions and updates happen in the developer's workflow, not via external dashboards.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Build plugins or extensions for popular IDEs that surface growth prompts, code suggestions, and auto-update flows based on code changes.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If your team uses highly custom or niche IDEs without plugin support, integration effort may outweigh benefits.

takeaways.title: Granular Knowledge Base Mapping
takeaways.insight: Maintain a highly granular, tech-stack-specific knowledge base of growth patterns and interventions. This enables precise, context-aware automation and competitive differentiation.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Tag guides and prompts by stack, tool, and use case (e.g., 'Astro + Firebase', 'Django + Stripe'). Use these tags for targeted retrieval and prompt generation.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If your product supports only a single stack or workflow, granular mapping may be overkill.

quick_wins: Segment your growth guides by tech stack for more targeted RAG retrieval.
Cache LLM outputs for common growth flows to reduce token usage.
Prototype a simple agent that runs activation logic in parallel with onboarding.
deeper_dives: Explore IDE plugin development for direct growth automation.
Research agentic architectures for orchestrating multiple codebase interventions.
Design versioned, code-first growth infrastructure to replace third-party scripts.

headlines.headline: Why Pre Skene is betting AGAINST UI overlays for PLG automation
headlines.subheadline: Instead of dashboards and popups, Pre Skene injects AI prompts straight into your IDE to automate onboarding and retention—will devs trust it?

headlines.headline: I reverse-engineered Pre Skene's AI prompts—here’s how they hijack your codebase for growth
headlines.subheadline: A deep dive into how Skene’s agentic architectures automate freemium gating and activation natively in your stack.

headlines.headline: Zapier for PLG, but in your IDE: Pre Skene’s bet on AI-native growth
headlines.subheadline: Skene bundles 100+ growth automations directly into developer workflows, skipping the UI layer entirely.

headlines.headline: The in-IDE AI prompt system every SaaS team should steal from Pre Skene
headlines.subheadline: Forget external dashboards—Skene’s natural-language-to-code agents automate activation and retention where devs actually work.

headlines.headline: Is Pre Skene just a clever feature, not a company? Let’s find out.
headlines.subheadline: With thin defensibility and a risky trust leap, Skene’s AI-powered growth automation faces a tough crowd in developer tools.

best_headline.headline: I reverse-engineered Pre Skene's AI prompts—here’s how they hijack your codebase for growth
best_headline.reason: This headline promises a technical teardown, appeals to engineers’ curiosity, and directly addresses the controversial trust leap and deep integration—while remaining honest about what Skene actually does.

viral_hooks.social_media_version: I reverse-engineered Pre Skene’s AI prompts—here’s how they automate growth inside your codebase.

hook.opening_line: What if your product could grow itself—right from your codebase?
hook.stakes: The next wave of developer tools won't just help you build faster—they'll automate how your product grows, retains, and monetizes users, turning growth into code.
hook.why_now: With AI-native workflows and developer-first PLG on the rise, the line between product and growth infrastructure is blurring—Skene is betting this is the future.

discovery.surface_story: Skene promises to automate onboarding, retention, and growth so developers can stay focused on shipping features, not fiddling with dashboards.
discovery.deeper_story: Under the hood, Skene is pioneering AI-powered prompts that interact directly with your codebase and IDE, embedding growth logic as first-class, versioned infrastructure—no more opaque third-party scripts.
discovery.surprise: Instead of a generic analytics overlay, Skene has built a massive, granular library of 381+ prompt-driven guides tailored to specific tech stacks and combinations, mapping growth playbooks directly to code-level interventions.

analysis.technical_insight: Skene leverages agentic architectures, RAG, and knowledge graphs to deliver growth automation as code, not UI—enabling granular, developer-owned control over activation and retention flows.
analysis.competitive_insight: While most PLG tools sit outside the product as black-box layers, Skene's approach is to make growth logic transparent, versioned, and developer-controlled—potentially redefining the category.
analysis.tension: Bull case: Skene becomes the default growth infrastructure layer for modern dev teams. Bear case: Without deep integrations or traction, it's just a clever feature—easily copied, hard to defend.

resolution.takeaway: Growth is shifting from dashboards to code—builders should prepare to own and automate these flows natively.
resolution.prediction: If Skene can prove trust and traction, it could spark a new wave of developer-first, AI-powered growth infra. If not, it risks being a footnote in the PLG tool wars.
resolution.call_to_action: Developers: Audit your growth stack. Where could automation and code-level control replace third-party scripts? Watch for tools that treat growth as infrastructure, not just analytics.

suggested_visuals: Diagram contrasting traditional PLG overlays vs Skene's code-integrated approach
Sample prompt-to-code flow for a common growth use case (e.g., onboarding in Django + Stripe)
Table mapping tech stacks to Skene's guide coverage

unique_voice_content:
```markdown
### Skene: Growth Automation That Actually Touches Your Code, Not Just Your Dashboard

Forget the usual parade of “growth automation” SaaS that pop up like mushrooms after rain, slapping dashboards and tracking scripts onto your product. Skene says: what if growth wasn’t a bolt-on, but baked right into your codebase—living in your IDE, version-controlled, and developer-owned? That’s a spicy claim. Let’s see if it holds up.

#### The Core Insight

Skene’s real twist is turning product-led growth (PLG) from a marketing layer into actual infrastructure—code, not dashboards. Imagine if onboarding flows, retention nudges, and freemium gates weren’t afterthoughts glued onto your UI, but part of your actual code, maintained like any other feature. It’s like replacing noisy third-party popups with logic you *own* and can reason about, right next to your main app logic. For devs tired of “magic tracking snippets” and opaque analytics, this is intriguing.

#### The Technical Meat

Architecturally, Skene leans hard on AI-powered prompts and agentic workflows. They read your codebase (with all the trust headaches that entails), generate growth loops, and push changes straight from your IDE. No browser overlays, no “connect your product” spaghetti—just code that lives where you work. The platform’s library—381+ guides with stack-specific prompts (Astro+Firebase, Django+Stripe, etc)—is a serious mapping effort, suggesting they’ve spent real time on the “last mile” developer experience. Their stack is modern: LangChain orchestration, Pinecone vectors, Anthropic/OpenAI for LLMs, all running on Railway/Render/self-hosted options.

But let’s be real: none of this is technically wild. Prompt libraries are copy-pasteable. Connecting to IDEs and codebases is table stakes for modern AI dev tools. The hardest part here is *getting developers to trust yet another agent crawling their source*, and convincing teams that “growth-as-code” is worth the cognitive overhead. Skene’s integration with Cursor and Claude Code is smart—hitching a ride with tools devs love—but it’s not enough to prevent incumbents (or open source) from rolling their own version in a hackathon weekend. There’s no proprietary data moat, no custom models, just clever bundling.

#### The Honest Assessment

Would I use this? If I’m running a SaaS with constant onboarding churn and I’m sick of wrangling third-party scripts—maybe. But I’d want to see proof that this actually *sticks* and doesn’t just add another layer of complexity. If you’re a growth PM who wants to ship experiments without bugging engineering, maybe this is magic. If you’re a dev who hates “magic,” approach with caution. This feels more like a feature than a platform—unless they nail deep integrations and prove real traction.

#### Builder Takeaway

- **Growth logic belongs in your code, not just your dashboards.** Think versioned, reviewable, and owned.
- **Prompt libraries are cheap; trust is expensive.** If your AI agent touches code, invest in transparency and developer control.
- **Integration is everything.** Hitch your wagon to the tools devs already use, but don’t count on that for defensibility.
- **Don’t confuse clever bundling with a real moat.** If it’s easy to copy, someone will.
- **Map out the “last mile.”** Stack-specific guides and real context pay dividends for adoption, but require grunt work.

#### Prediction

Skene will either get snapped up by a bigger dev tool (Cursor, Replit, Sourcegraph, etc.) or become a footnote unless they show crazy adoption or build a real moat. If they ship genuine proprietary data or platform lock-in—*and* earn developer trust—they’ll have my attention.
```
